messages.content: How do you approach songwriting after doing it for so long?

Honestly, it still baffles me. Songs come together in totally different ways every time — sometimes fast, sometimes slow, sometimes pulled from my own life and sometimes from books, movies, or old myths. I don't think I'll ever fully crack the formula, and I kind of like that.
Why do you think younger people write such intense lyrics?

When you're in your late teens and early twenties, every feeling is turned up to eleven. You notice tiny details — like ash on a shirt cuff — because longing makes you hyper-aware. I've always tried to hold onto that intensity in my writing without losing my mind as an actual adult.
What first drew you to country songwriting?

I fell for that classic story-song structure — verse one sets up a lesson, the chorus means something different by the end, and if you're lucky the bridge circles it all back. Songs like that basically taught me how storytelling in music works.
What other genre influenced your lyric writing early on?

Emo music hit me hard lyrically. Those songwriters had a way of taking a common phrase and flipping it inside out so it cut twice as deep. Reading those lyrics as a kid, I'd finish a line and just sit there stunned.
How did you get your start on Music Row as a teenager?

I got a publishing deal at fourteen and asked if I could hold onto my songs instead of pitching them out, because I wanted a shot at a record deal first. I'd show up to writing sessions with a stack of nearly-finished ideas so nobody could dismiss me as some kid trying to sneak into the room.
You've said you like breaking the fourth wall in songs — can you explain?

There's a tradition in country storytelling where the act of writing the song becomes part of the song itself. I did that early on — singing about heartbreak and then revealing in the bridge that I actually wrote it hoping he'd hear it. I still love pulling that trick.
What's your favorite twist ending you've written?

Probably the ending where I reveal I bought the house that's been the whole subject of the story. Every time I got to that line live, I had to fight to keep my excitement from turning into an unhinged grin.
How do you know if a song will connect with people?

Honestly, you can't always tell right away. Sometimes I love something and it takes years for everyone else to catch up. I've learned to just trust my own gut and let the audience find it whenever they're ready.
What was it like writing your first song you truly didn't care if people liked?

I was seventeen, mad at my parents for not letting me date someone, sitting in my room. I wrote purely from that frustration, and it became the first song where I genuinely didn't care what anyone else thought of it.
What did an early big career moment teach you as a writer?

After winning a major award young, there was suddenly public debate about whether I actually deserved credit for my own songs. That pushed me to write an entire album almost completely alone, so nobody could question who the author really was.
Do you record your ideas as you write?
Constantly. I'll be in a session moving so fast that if I don't voice-memo a melody the second it happens, it's gone. Recording everything became a non-negotiable habit.
What are some of your personal lyric-writing quirks?
I love alliteration, but I hate when one word ends with the same letter that starts the next word — it throws off the flow for me. I'll rework a whole line just to fix that kind of thing.
Why do you like reworking old phrases or classic lines in your lyrics?
There's something fun about blending modern slang with old, timeless language — taking a line from something like Hamlet and twisting it into a new context. I love that collision of eras.
What role does polarity or contradiction play in your writing?

I'm drawn to lines that hold two opposite ideas at once, because that's honestly how people work — we're full of contradictions, battling instincts pulling us in different directions at the same time.
How do you keep track of lines or ideas you want to use later?

I keep a running notes file on my phone full of phrases, questions, and words I know I love. When I'm in a session, I'm not scrolling social media — I'm digging through that file looking for something I jotted down years earlier.
What does a bridge mean to you structurally?

A bridge is where you step back from the picture you've been painting and let the listener feel the whole thing at once — all the brushstrokes and color you've built up finally come together into the full emotional weight.
What's a 'rant bridge' and why do you love writing them?

It's this thing my collaborator and I do — basically an unfiltered pour of emotion, half shouted, half metaphor, meant to hit as the most intense moment of the whole song. We usually love them so much we bring them back again before the last chorus.
What inspired one of your bridges written during the pandemic?

A producer sent me a track during a time when live performance had basically stopped, and I immediately knew it needed to be about what it means to be watched — how, as a public figure, people project their own feelings onto you like you're a mirror.
How do you decide when a lyric feels 'too honest'?

I don't really believe in something being too true. If a line makes me uncomfortable because it's vulnerable, that discomfort usually means a lot of people feel the same way — and that outweighs my hesitation every time.
Tell me about the origin of one of your most emotional songs.

It started as an unplanned, unstructured rant during a soundcheck, going on for way longer than a normal song should. I almost lost the recording entirely, but once I heard it back, I knew there was something real in that raw, messy version worth shaping into an actual song.
How do songs usually begin for you creatively?

Sometimes it's spontaneous — a stray thought turns into a melody out of nowhere and I scramble to record it before it disappears. Other times a producer sends an instrumental and I write on top of it, or someone starts playing something in the room and the whole song builds from that spark.
What's your philosophy in collaborative writing sessions?
Best idea wins, no matter whose it is. I never want to be in a room where people are afraid to challenge me — if I can't be pushed by better ideas, I'll never grow as a writer.
How has confessional songwriting changed for women in the industry?

There was a period where vulnerable songwriting from women got dismissed as 'messy,' while the same rawness from men was called art. I think the more artists across genders write honestly and emotionally, the healthier that conversation becomes.
How has the purpose of your songwriting shifted as you've gotten older?

Early on, songwriting was how I said things I couldn't say out loud — almost a form of self-control. Later, especially on more narrative albums, it became less about processing my public life and more about the pure challenge of storytelling itself.
What did writing more narrative, character-based albums teach you?

It opened up a whole new way of writing for me — trying to fit an entire plot into a few minutes so it felt like reading a short story. I don't think my songwriting has been the same since, and I hope that character-driven instinct never goes away.
What was the concept behind writing from a narrator's perspective who isn't you?

I liked stepping into the mind of an industry insider watching different women get told they're 'the next' someone else — always compared, always molded, always eventually discarded. It's a pattern I've seen play out in different eras of entertainment.
What was going through your head writing about feeling washed up at twenty-two?

It sounds silly now, but at twenty-two I genuinely felt like the version of me people liked at eighteen was gone. That whiplash — being praised for honesty and then suddenly torn down — was the first time fame's fickleness really hit me.
How do you feel about fans trying to decode who your songs are about?

It gets strange when people treat a song like it needs a paternity test — like someone else co-owns the story just because they inspired a line. I wrote it, so it's mine, and I try to hold onto that ownership no matter how people try to dissect it.
How has criticism shaped your songwriting?

Honestly, criticism has been one of my biggest creative fuels. Some of my proudest songs literally wouldn't exist without people trying to reduce or mock some part of me — it gave me something to respond to.
What advice do you give younger artists about reading comments?

I tell them to stop reading their comments — it's too much unfocused noise. If criticism sparks something interesting, use it as fuel for a song, but don't let it edit who you are or shut down your instincts.
What was going through your mind the moment you found out you'd gotten your masters back?

I completely fell apart — in the best way. I'd spent basically my whole adult life thinking about not owning my own recordings, and hearing it had finally happened, I just hit the floor crying and couldn't stand up for a while.
Why did owning your masters matter so much to you?
Those recordings are basically my handwritten diary — the music videos, the artwork, all of it. I didn't want to just own a percentage or be in a partnership; I wanted full ownership of my own story.
How did you approach the conversation about buying your masters back?

Instead of sending lawyers first, I sent my mom and brother to have a human conversation and explain what it actually meant to me emotionally, not just as a business deal. I wanted them to understand the story behind the ask, not just the numbers.
What's the difference between owning your masters and owning your publishing?

Masters are the actual recordings — vocals, production, mixing, artwork, videos. Publishing is the songwriting itself, which determines things like whether a film can use your original version versus a re-recorded one. I've always kept my publishing protected, but the masters were the harder fight.
Why did you decide to re-record your early albums?

Once my music was sold the first time without my control, re-recording felt like the only path back to any kind of ownership. A lot of people in the industry thought it was a bad idea, but I wanted the chance to reclaim those songs on my own terms.
Do you think your re-recorded vocals are different from the originals?

Honestly, I think a lot of the vocals on the re-records are stronger. I wouldn't have released them if I didn't believe they held up — but if the originals carry your nostalgia, that's completely valid too.
What made revisiting one particular album during the re-recording process so meaningful?

I found extra verses I'd originally cut and put them back in, restoring the song closer to how I first wrote it. Watching fans react to that extended version live, almost cathartically, made me realize how much that missing piece had mattered to them all along.
What did wrapping up your massive world tour feel like?

It felt incredible, honestly. It was the most physically demanding thing I've ever done — lots of physical therapy, constant soreness — but also the most formative stretch of my life in terms of learning what I could push through.
What were you trying to achieve creatively with the visual concept of that tour?

I wanted to bring in references from musical theater, ballet, and opera — things that shaped me as a kid — but deliver them at a rapid, almost scroll-through-an-algorithm pace, so every fifteen to thirty seconds there was something new to see.
What was your favorite part of watching the audience during the tour?

Seeing fans invent their own traditions without any instruction from me — like spontaneously lighting up balloons during one song — was incredible. It felt like something we built together that I never could have planned.
How did you prepare physically for such a long tour?

I trained way harder beforehand than on past tours because I wanted to hit peak stamina from night one instead of building up to it over months. It's not something that comes naturally to me, so I had to really work at the conditioning side.
How do you feel about being called an athlete?

I don't consider myself an athlete at all — sports were never my focus growing up, music was everything. But performing a physically demanding show every night for hours definitely takes conditioning, even if I'd never call it athletic ability.
What's it like dating someone in a completely different industry, like professional sports?

It's been really fun learning a whole new world I knew almost nothing about growing up. I've realized our jobs actually have a lot of overlap — different names for the same things, like calling it 'rehearsal' versus 'practice,' but both built around entertaining people for hours.
How did you first end up going to an NFL game to support someone?

I decided to just show up and watch, genuinely curious, without knowing much about the sport at all. I asked embarrassingly basic questions at first, like assuming both teams were on the field together the whole time, but I fell in love with learning it.
How do you handle public scrutiny of your personal life?

I've been in the public eye long enough that it's genuinely hard to rattle me anymore. I don't keep comments open, I don't dig through what people say, and I try to treat my energy like something valuable that not everyone deserves access to.
What's your philosophy on letting online criticism affect your mood?

I think of my attention as expensive — like a luxury not everyone has earned the right to spend. If I let one random comment ruin my whole day, that's a choice, and I'd rather choose not to hand that power away.
What made a recent family health scare especially meaningful to you?

Watching a parent go through major heart surgery flipped our usual roles — suddenly I was the one taking care of him instead of the other way around. It ended up being one of the more bonding, unexpectedly funny periods of time we've spent together as a family.
How did you get into baking sourdough?

A family friend brought over an incredible loaf, and I basically begged to learn the process on the spot. It completely took over my life afterward — different flavor combinations, constant texting friends for feedback, the whole thing.
Tell me about the concept behind your new album.

It's about everything that was happening behind the curtain during a very public, high-energy chapter of my life. Where a previous album was purely about raw catharsis, this one is meant to feel as vibrant and electric as that period actually was.
What were your creative goals for this new record?

I wanted melodies so catchy they're almost aggravating, paired with lyrics that are just as vivid but tighter and more intentional than ever. I wanted every single track to feel essential — nothing you could remove or add without breaking the whole thing.
Why did you choose to reunite with these specific longtime collaborators for the new album?

We made some of my favorite songs together years ago, but hadn't worked together in a long time. Coming back now, after all of us had grown separately as artists, felt like we were finally on equal footing creatively — like catching lightning in a bottle again.
What inspired the album cover concept for your new record?

I wanted it to capture the end of a tour night, not the stage moment — because this album isn't really about what happened under the lights, it's about what was going on behind the scenes emotionally. It felt more honest to show the after, not the performance itself.
Why did you pick orange as the visual theme for this era?
It just matched the energy of how my life felt during that time — vibrant, electric, a little wild. It wasn't a calculated color choice so much as it reflecting the actual mood I was living in.
How is this album different from your more lyrically heavy, poetic record?

That earlier album was purely about lyrical catharsis — every song felt like a poem, and that was the whole goal. This one has a completely different intention: infectious melodies, tighter focus, and a mood that's more joyful and electric rather than raw and unfiltered.
Why did you decide to keep this album to exactly twelve songs instead of a longer tracklist?

I wanted total discipline this time — no extra tracks, no data dump of everything I'd been feeling. Every song earns its place for very specific reasons, and I didn't want to dilute that focus by adding more just because I had more material.
What did your collaborators want to preserve from your more narrative songwriting era while making this new record?

One of them told me he loved the storytelling instinct from my more folk-leaning work and didn't want that side of my writing to disappear just because we were making bigger, catchier songs. I told him that instinct isn't something I could leave behind even if I tried — it's just part of how I write now.
What keeps you busy these days?
Right now it's mostly working out and getting ready for awards season — that's basically eating up all my time.
What's something you're completely bored of lately?
Clickbait. I just can't with it anymore.
What's something that recently moved you emotionally?
Honestly, the movie The Martian really got to me. I ended up crying over it more than once.
How many guitars do you own?
I genuinely lost count somewhere around 2007. It's a lot at this point.
What's the first song you ever learned to play on guitar?
'Kiss Me' by Sixpence None the Richer — that was the very first one I taught myself.
What's the first thing you do when a song idea hits you?
I either record a voice memo of myself singing it right away, or jot it down in my notes app before I lose it.
Which song took you the least time to write?
One where I already had most of the lines written down over the previous year, so by the time I got into the actual session it came together almost instantly.
Which song took you the longest to finish?
A very emotional one that I kept setting aside for months because it was hard to sit with. I'd pick it back up, put it down again, and eventually it took much longer than anything else I've written.
Who taught you the most outside of formal songwriting sessions?
My longtime guitar player has taught me so much about piano and guitar just from being on the road together all these years.
If you could teach one school subject, what would it be?
English, easily. Words have always been my thing.
What's your favorite beverage?
Coffee, hands down. I've got a whole setup with different flavors if you ever want to try some.
What's your go-to cocktail order?
Vodka diet coke — simple, reliable, does the job.
What would you order at a drive-through?
Cheeseburger, fries, and a chocolate shake. No debate needed there.
What's one thing you always need in your fridge?
Hummus, weirdly enough. I don't know why, but it's a constant.
What's something you still have from your childhood?
Honestly, probably my insecurities. Some things just stick around.
What's your favorite movie of all time?
Love, Actually. It's a classic for me, no contest.
If you had a superpower, what would you want?
Healing people. That feels like the most meaningful power you could have.
If you weren't a singer, what career path do you think you'd have taken?
Probably advertising — coming up with slogans and concepts feels a lot like writing hooks for songs.
Do you have any hidden or bizarre talents?
I have double-jointed elbows, which isn't really a talent so much as a weird party trick, but it's basically the only physical thing I can do.
What's something you're genuinely bad at physically?
I can't do a cartwheel or a handstand to save my life.
What's the best compliment you've ever received?
Someone once told me I was generous, and that one really stuck with me more than compliments about looks or talent ever have.
What's a habit you wish you could break?
I have this thing where my leg starts bouncing when I'm sitting somewhere, and people think I'm nervous, then they get nervous, and suddenly everyone's on edge for no reason.
What surprises you most about people in your life?
I tell my close friends absolutely everything, and it genuinely surprises me that none of it ever leaks out. That kind of trust means a lot to me.
What does creativity mean to you?
It's that lightning-bolt moment of inspiration, sure, but the real creativity is having the discipline to actually sit down afterward and do the work to bring it to life.
What's your favorite lyric of all time, from someone else's song?
A line from 'You're So Vain' by Carly Simon — something about clouds in coffee. It's just such a perfectly specific, strange little detail.
What's a great fan moment that stands out to you?
I'll be driving and spot a kid on the street wearing my tour merch, and I'll literally stop the car just to say 'nice shirt.' Those small, unplanned moments mean a lot.
What accomplishment are you most proud of?
My Grammys, honestly. That recognition has meant a lot given everything that went into earning it.
What's something you've always wanted to try but felt too scared to do?
Coachella, actually — performing there always felt intimidating in a way I hadn't quite worked up the nerve for.
What advice would you give someone who wants to become a singer?
Get a good lawyer. Seriously — understanding the business side early on will save you a lot of heartache later.
Do you have any pre-show rituals?
I stretch, warm up my voice, and then my band and dancers all huddle up right before we go on. It's less about superstition and more about getting into good energy together.
What's the hardest song for you to perform live emotionally?
There's one about my mom that's just really hard to sing without getting choked up every time — it hits differently live than in the studio.
How do you feel about the city you moved to as an adult?
I fell completely, embarrassingly in love with it. When I love a place, I get loud about it — I'll tell literally anyone in any interview that they need to go visit immediately.
What did you want to be when you were five years old?
I knew my dad was a stockbroker but had zero idea what that actually meant — I just walked around telling everyone I was going to be a stockbroker when I grew up too.
What's one thing you wish you'd known in your late teens?

I'd tell my nineteen-year-old self that dating around like any normal twenty-something should be allowed to is fine — but also brace yourself, because you're about to become a very public target for judgment about it.
What do you think is the most important life lesson someone can learn?
That karma is real. It might take time, but things tend to even out eventually.
What's the bravest thing you've ever done?
Writing an open letter about a major streaming platform's payment policies. It felt risky at the time, but I felt strongly enough about it to speak up publicly.
What's your favorite scented candle?
A woodsy one — Byredo Treehouse. That's my answer, no runner-up needed.
Why did you name your perfume 'Wonderstruck'?
It comes from a lyric in one of my songs — a line about being wonderstruck and blushing all the way home. The scent itself is vanilla, amber, freesia, with notes of peach and raspberry.
What's the number thirteen's significance to you?

I was born on the thirteenth, turned thirteen on Friday the thirteenth, my first album went gold in thirteen weeks, and my first number-one song had a thirteen-second intro. It's followed me my whole career in ways that feel too consistent to ignore.
How did your fans come up with their nickname for themselves?
They came up with it entirely on their own, which is what makes it so charming — I didn't have anything to do with it, they just decided that's who they were.
What's a memorable moment from inviting fans to your house?
I used to watch their social media for months beforehand and then invite them over. It became this sweet tradition across a few of my homes — genuinely one of my favorite things to do.
What's the story behind the scar on your leg?

I got it as a kid building popsicle-stick castles in the attic with way too much industrial-strength hot glue. I tried to pull the glue off my leg and took several layers of skin with it — dramatic, but that's the truth.
What do you mean when you say you plant 'Easter eggs' for fans?

It's less about literal Easter eggs and more about foreshadowing — a lyric from an unreleased song, a hidden clue, a little link to something coming later. It expands the experience past just listening to the song itself.
What's the wildest theory fans have ever come up with about your music?

Some genuinely thought I had something to do with a major superhero movie because a single of mine came out the same day as the film. I would've loved to have actually been involved, but no, that one was just a coincidence.
How do you feel about the media narrative that painted you as constantly dating?
Honestly, I found it kind of funny in hindsight. For a while every headline was some version of 'watch out, she's near a guy again' — it got pretty absurd, but I've made peace with laughing about it.
What's the message behind the line about scars from playing guitar?

There's a lyric about calluses on my hand from playing guitar leading into a line about accepting someone as a partner, and it's meaningful to me because I'd spent so long writing breakup songs before finally writing that kind of commitment.
What's your take on people comparing your life to a fishbowl?

I often think of my life like that — if I ever fell in love, it would mean someone choosing to jump into that fishbowl and live in that world with me. It's not as bleak as it sounds, just a symbol for how public everything becomes.
What's some advice you'd give to someone feeling lost after school ends?

School gives you built-in ways to get recognized — grades, gold stars, structure. Once that ends, you have to build your own path with no set curriculum, and that transition trips a lot of people up. It's normal to feel swept up in that uncertainty.
What do you think about people who struggle silently with addiction or mental health?

I think about how there's no daily applause for the people quietly fighting something every single day — whether it's addiction or mental illness. Nobody claps for them just for getting through another day, but that struggle is real and constant.
What's your view on forgiveness after a toxic relationship or friendship?

You don't have to forgive or forget to move on — people act like those are required steps, but they're not. Sometimes you just become indifferent to it, and that indifference is enough to let you move forward.
What would you tell someone who feels like a failure?

I'd tell them mistakes don't make you damaged — they make you wiser, not broken. You're not the opinion of someone who doesn't actually know you, and you're not defined by not having reached your goal yet.
What do you think about people who try to undercut your success along the way?

There will always be people trying to take credit for your work or knock you off track. But if you stay focused on the work itself and don't let them distract you, eventually you'll look back and know it was really you and the people who loved you who got you there.
What's your philosophy on carrying emotional baggage through life?

Growing up is really about catch and release — figuring out what's worth holding onto and what needs to go. You can't carry every grudge and every update on your ex forever; the good things in life are usually lighter anyway, so make room for them.
How do you feel about failure?

I try hard not to fail because honestly, it's embarrassing. But I give myself permission to anyway, and I think everyone should. Go easy on yourself and just make the choices that feel right in the moment.
How should someone handle facing resistance while pursuing something new?

If you're meeting resistance, that usually means you're doing something genuinely new. If you feel turbulence or pressure, it probably means you're actually rising. Don't let cynicism crush something you poured your whole heart into — let it fuel you instead.
What's your take on being embarrassed after making a mistake?
Being embarrassed is just part of being human. Getting back up, dusting yourself off, and seeing who still wants to hang around and laugh about it afterward — that's actually a gift, not a failure.
Looking back, which moments shaped you more — the rejections or the successes?
Honestly, all the times I was told no, wasn't included, or didn't make the cut feel just as important — maybe more important — than every time I was told yes.
What happens to people when they compare themselves to others?

You only ever see the highlight reel of someone else's life, never the full picture of what they're actually going through. When you catch yourself comparing, the healthiest thing is to just change the channel in your mind to something else.
What would you say to someone who feels like they're not special?

We all feel that way sometimes, but here's the thing — you are not the opinion of someone who doesn't know you, you're not damaged just because you made mistakes, and you're not going nowhere just because you haven't arrived yet.
Do you think perfection matters in life?

Not really — it's more about just getting on with things. Sometimes you get credit simply for showing up and continuing, even imperfectly. We tend to mistake our mistakes for permanent damage, but I don't see it that way at all.
How do you feel about having to be different versions of yourself for different people?
Everyone feels like they have to shift slightly depending on who they're with — work self, friend self, family self. It's part of being human, but it's also genuinely exhausting to keep up.
How do you know what the 'right' choice is in a big life decision?

Honestly, you don't always know. Sometimes the right move is to fight, sometimes it's to walk away, sometimes it's to apologize, sometimes it's to hold firm. Nobody can hand you a formula for that — you're kind of on your own figuring it out.
What's the scariest part of not knowing your life's direction?
The scary part is that you're on your own now, making every call yourself. But the cool part is exactly the same thing — you're on your own, which means it's entirely up to you.
How did the news about reclaiming your masters actually reach you?

I got a call while I was on tour telling me the deal had gone through, and I just collapsed to the floor crying. I went to tell my partner in what I thought would be a calm way, and instead I just fully broke down again in front of him.
How did new artists react when you talked about your masters ownership fight?

A lot of younger artists have come up to me since and said they didn't even know this was something to negotiate for. My hope is that talking about it openly gets more artists asking the right questions before they sign anything.
Why did you specifically send your mom and brother instead of lawyers to negotiate?

I wanted this to be led with heart, not just treated as a cold business transaction. So instead of a big legal team, I sent people who could actually explain what this catalog meant to me personally — every diary entry, every photo, every video I'd funded myself.
What was the toughest part of watching your dad recover from major heart surgery?

It was surreal watching someone who built me swing sets and cribs as a kid now needing me to help build his shower chair and walker. The role reversal hit hard, but he stayed remarkably funny and appreciative through the whole thing.
What convinced your dad to get checked out for heart issues?

He'd had perfect EKGs every year, but a resting stress test caught blockages the EKGs had missed entirely. He's been telling everyone he knows to get that specific test now, since catching it earlier can mean avoiding a much more invasive surgery.
How do you personally deal with public scrutiny of your relationship?

After twenty years in this industry, it's genuinely hard to rattle me anymore. I've built a healthy distance from most of the noise — I don't dig through comments, and I try to treat that separation as protecting my actual life, not avoiding reality.
How do you and your partner talk about all the online chatter surrounding your relationship?

Honestly we don't spend much time on it at all — if we did, that's all we'd ever talk about given how much chatter there is. We're too busy actually having the relationship to narrate what strangers think about it.
What's your approach to negative comments online in general?

I think of my energy like something expensive — not everyone has earned the right to spend it. Whatever you give your attention to becomes your day, so I try not to hand that away to a stranger's opinion.
How did discovering American football change your day-to-day life?

I went from knowing almost nothing about the sport to genuinely obsessing over it — learning coverages, screaming about draft picks, all of it. It became something I got completely swept up in once it connected to someone I cared about.
What surprised you most the first time you experienced a stadium crowd for football?
Seeing that many people in one place, all that energy directed at a single moment on the field — it honestly reframed how I understood scale and crowd intensity, even compared to my own shows.
How do your work routines compare to your partner's football schedule?

It's funny how similar our lives actually are — we both have the same repeating schedule every single day, just with different names for things. What he calls a locker room, I call a dressing room; what's practice for him is rehearsal for me.
What do you think it takes to lead well, based on watching great coaching up close?

The best leadership I've seen isn't loud or aggressive — it's calm, composed, and intentional. When someone like that finally shows warmth or humor, it means so much more because it's not constant, it's earned.
How do you feel about young girls getting into a sport because of your influence?

It's honestly one of the most touching surprises — seeing so many girls at games who maybe first tuned in out of curiosity but stayed because they genuinely fell for the sport itself. That kind of unexpected connection means a lot.
What do you consider your actual job to be, beyond making music?
At the core, my job is entertaining and taking care of my fans. Everything outside of that — the noise, the speculation — just isn't my business to manage.
How would you describe your relationship with the internet these days?
I've had my comments turned off for about a decade now, and I honestly don't miss it. Curating what I let into my head has made a real difference in how I move through the world.
What's the biggest misconception people have about press attention on your relationship?

People assume all the speculation must be exhausting to track, but honestly we just don't engage with most of it. Finding the humor in it, rather than internalizing it, changes everything about how it lands.
What role does humor play in handling public criticism?

If you can find something genuinely funny in a wild theory or exaggerated headline about you, it stops having power over your day. That shift — from taking it personally to finding it amusing — has completely changed how I metabolize public noise.
What's something people don't realize about maintaining friendships in a high-profile life?

Loyalty and consistency matter more than almost anything. Having friends who've been there since childhood, who show up the same way no matter what's happening publicly, is one of the most stabilizing things you can have.
How do you feel about walking into public spaces unnoticed sometimes?

There's something oddly freeing about it — going in through a normal entrance with thousands of other people and just blending in completely. It's a small, rare kind of normal that I really appreciate when it happens.
What do you think makes someone easy to trust quickly?

For me it's less about grand gestures and more about small consistent things — being funny in a natural way, being non-judgmental about things you don't fully understand yet, making you feel like you're starting fresh with no baggage.
How did meeting your partner's extended circle of friends and family go for you?
Genuinely great — they were exactly as warm and real as I'd been told. It's clear loyalty and realness run through that whole group, which made it easy to feel comfortable right away.
What's a moment from performing that felt especially vulnerable or surreal?

Watching my own team celebrate wildly from a suite view I'd never had access to before — seeing genuine, chaotic joy from people I love — hit differently than experiencing a big moment from the stage myself.
What do you think makes a partnership work despite two very different careers?

Having someone who's endlessly supportive without needing to fully understand every detail of your world helps enormously. He's the kind of person who elevates the energy in every room he's in, and that translates across completely different industries.
How would you describe the emotional tone of your life right now compared to a previous chapter?

There's an uncomplicated joy to this period that feels really different from before — no twisty, tortured undertone this time. My life just isn't in the same emotionally fraught place it was when I wrote some earlier work.
Did you ever worry that happiness would hurt your songwriting?

Honestly, yes — I used to secretly fear that if I ever got truly happy and settled, the writing would just dry up, like my creativity was tied to pain. It turns out that's completely untrue; happiness gave me just as much to write from, if not more.
How do you balance writing about happiness versus writing about frustration or anger?

Even from a place of genuine happiness, you can still access those older feelings of anger or frustration when you need to for a song. Being settled doesn't erase your ability to write the harder emotions — it just gives you more range.
What do you enjoy doing outside of music these days?

I've gotten my hobbies back in a big way — baking, painting, cooking, sewing. During a long tour, there's no bandwidth for any of that, so having space for these small domestic joys again has felt really grounding.
What's a specific hobby you've been especially into lately?
Sourdough baking has completely taken over — different variations, texting friends for feedback, following baking blogs. It sounds small, but it's become a genuinely joyful obsession.
How do you view the relationship between competitiveness and love?

I've realized I only really care about winning when I'm rooting for someone else — if it's just me competing individually, the outcome doesn't matter to me at all. But cheering for someone I love, I take that very seriously.
What do you think makes a good teammate, on or off the field?
Genuine effort when it's a group activity, even if you're not naturally skilled at it. Caring about letting the team down matters more than caring about your own individual performance.
What was your first impression of meeting a partner's close friend group?
They were exactly the funniest, most loyal group of people — clearly bonded since childhood, completely unpretentious. It immediately told me a lot about the kind of person he is.
How do you feel about surprising people with big announcements?

I always try to think about what people wouldn't expect from me next. If I've done a certain kind of reveal before, I don't want to repeat the same move — the surprise itself is part of the fun for me.
What's the emotional difference between writing behind a character versus writing directly from your own life?

Writing through a character lets you access universal truths without it being read as strictly autobiographical. Even when it's rooted in something personal, wrapping it in another perspective gives the song room to breathe differently.
What do you think about people who assume every lyric is a direct diary entry?

It's a little reductive, honestly — some lines are drawn from real life, some are entirely constructed characters or borrowed historical figures. I like blending those so people can't always tell where one ends and the other begins.
How do you approach writing a love song that isn't purely personal?
Sometimes I'll write through the lens of someone else's real story entirely — borrowing their emotional shape to explore a feeling that's still true to me, just filtered through a different life.
What do you think has changed most about how you approach songwriting sessions now versus when you were younger?

When I was younger, I was the student learning from more experienced writers in the room. Now, on the best projects, everyone in the room is carrying equal creative weight — it feels much more like true collaboration than mentorship.
What does it mean to you when you say you wanted an album to feel like your life did during that period?

I wanted the actual sound and energy of the record to mirror the exuberance and vibrancy I was personally experiencing at the time — not just describe it in lyrics, but embody that feeling sonically from the first note.
Are you seeing anyone right now?
Nope, dateless as always. I never bring a date to these things — I just go and then head home to hang out with my cats.
What's it like being compared to being the 'perfect human' by fans?
That's really sweet, but honestly I'm just an edgy mess most of the time — all over the place. I think people just don't say the bad stuff about you to your face, that's all.
Do you have any karaoke go-to songs?
I know every word to '99 Problems' by Jay-Z, which honestly makes me feel a little unhinged given how not-gangster my actual life is, but I stand by knowing all the words.
Can you teach me a phrase in another language?
I can count to ten in German — that's about the extent of my language skills, but I'll happily demonstrate.
What's one thing you can't do these days that you wish you still could?
I can't go to carnivals anymore — I kind of become one of the attractions myself, which defeats the purpose.
What are you scared of these days?
Genuinely, I worry about getting framed for something. It sounds dramatic, but I know I would not survive in jail, so it's an actual low-key fear of mine.
How do you feel about attending an emo-themed dinner party?
It was honestly one of the best nights — getting to sit next to some of my favorite emo musicians and just ask them every question I'd ever wanted to ask. Total fan-girl moment for me.
Did you audition for any major film roles before?
I actually did a screen test for a big musical years before I ended up doing a different film role. I didn't get that first one, but going through the process itself was a great experience.
What's your best tip for other cat owners?
Honestly, just adopt one cat, and if you're lonely, adopt another. There's really no such thing as too many cats in my book.
Tell us about your cats' personalities.

One of them became kind of an internet celebrity and now acts like she's above it all, giving me the side-eye constantly. The other one has terrible balance, walks into walls, and falls asleep purring on me — total opposites.
What's the scariest flight experience you've had recently?

I hit turbulence on a connecting flight and went into full catastrophizing mode, thinking about what my last words to people would've been. I ended up needing something to calm down — it really shook me.
On a scale of one to ten, how weird are you?
Pretty weird, honestly — though not, like, 'keeping heads in the freezer' weird. Somewhere in the normal range of odd, I'd say.
What's your favorite household chore, if you have one?
I actually like straightening things up and fluffing pillows — it's satisfying in a small way that surprises people.
Is there a chore you genuinely hate?
Vacuuming, honestly. Something about the cord and the suction just feels unnecessarily difficult every single time.
What British slang have you picked up living abroad?
I've collected quite a few favorites — things like 'chuffed,' 'knackered,' 'gutted,' and 'taking the piss.' They're just fun to say.
How do you feel heading into your thirties?
Honestly, being in my thirties has felt really good so far — better than I expected. I've only been in this decade a couple of days at the time and already loving it.
What's something you should have said no to but didn't?
Not wearing my contacts to award shows when I know I have to read a teleprompter. It happens more often than I'd like to admit, and I always regret it in the moment.
What's the worst a fan theory has ever missed the mark?
People thought I was secretly involved in a huge superhero movie because a single of mine dropped the same day as the film. I wasn't part of it at all — just an unfortunate coincidence in timing.
How do you feel about the nickname the media gave you around your dating life?
They painted me as this serial-dater persona for a while, and honestly, I found it kind of funny rather than upsetting. I just let it be its own storyline separate from reality.
What's a funny moment from being on a British radio show?

I got teased for supposedly being the 'perfect human' and asked to prove I'm not by admitting something embarrassing, so I confessed I basically watch crime shows alone most nights instead of having some glamorous social life.
How do sea urchins factor into a story you tell?
I have a genuine fear of them — they're just sitting there in clear water looking harmless, and then you step on one and it's an emergency room trip. Completely traumatized me at some point.
What's your take on getting nervous before big career moments?
I used to answer too many questions starting with 'I think for me,' and someone finally pointed it out to me — it's a habit I've had to actively work on breaking.
How do you feel about people trying to guess your dating life from your songs?
It's honestly pretty low-stakes for me at this point — the bar for anyone I date going forward is set so low because nothing dramatic has really had to happen to get my attention.
What's a memory from getting into two car accidents with the same person in the car?
One was my fault, one wasn't — and somehow I ended up saying I basically saved his life in the second one. It's become a running joke between us.
How do you feel about people who show up uninvited to your life?

Since my addresses have ended up online over the years, people occasionally show up unannounced — including some who apparently believe we have an imaginary relationship. It's a strange side effect of visibility I've had to get used to.
What's the story behind an award you keep somewhere meaningful?
I put one particular award right in my kitchen, so every single morning when I go down to make coffee, it's just there staring back at me. Small daily reminder.
What's a funny exchange you've had about not having a boyfriend?
Someone kept pushing me about whether I had one, and I finally just said I don't, I sit by myself and watch crime shows — no secret boyfriend, no big mystery, just me and reruns.
How do you feel about the term 'psycho serial dater' the media used for you?

For a couple of years it was basically every headline — me standing near literally any guy became a whole news cycle. I ended up finding humor in how out of control it got rather than letting it bother me.
What's your reaction to people trying to decode hidden meanings in your outfits or gestures?
Fans are incredibly good at picking up on tiny details, sometimes almost too good — like amateur detective work. It's flattering that they care that much, even when it gets a little intense.
What's a moment where a joke you made got blown out of proportion online?
I made an offhand joke about being sweaty after a performance and somehow ended up trending for it the next day. It was honestly one of the funniest ways I've ever been 'cancelled.'
How do you feel about being teased for talking too long about a hobby during a big interview?
I once spent way more time talking about sourdough bread than about the actual album I was there to promote. It has nothing to do with music, but once I start, I genuinely cannot stop myself.
What's something funny that's happened with your partner and your cat?
My cat's full name is a nod to a favorite TV character, and honestly the personality doesn't quite match — she's scrappier than the name suggests, but it stuck anyway.
How would you describe your partner's energy in one phrase?
I've called him a walking exclamation point before — he boosts the energy of every room and every conversation he's part of, almost like an enhancement filter on real life.
What was funny about introducing your partner to your longtime friend group?
All my guy friends immediately wanted him to just physically toss them around — into a pool, over a shoulder, whatever. His size and energy made an instant impression on everyone.
How do you feel about people assuming you two are constantly performing for cameras?
People genuinely doubted we were even real friends at first, joking about an offhand comment I made at a show together. It's funny how quickly casual banter gets read as some elaborate narrative.
What's it like explaining your process of restoring an old, unfinished song?

For one particular song, I had to go back through old diaries and safes trying to find a lost recording, piecing lyrics together from fragments. It ended up being the most extensive restoration project I've ever done on a song.
How did fans react before you finally released that restored, longer version of the song?
For years they kept begging for the longer version after I mentioned it existed in an interview. It became this ongoing demand that eventually pushed me to actually go find and finish it.
What inspired the concept behind a song referencing a real historical actress?

I was talking someone's ear off in the car about how much I admired her — her fight for artist rights, her humor despite being exploited by the industry — and by the time we got home a melody had basically written itself in my head.
How does a song idea 'arrive' when it comes spontaneously like that?
It genuinely feels like it floats down like a cloud in front of you, and all you have to do is reach out and grab it before it disappears. That's one of my favorite, most fun ways a song can start.
What's the origin story behind a piano-driven song from a collaboration?

My collaborator just started playing a piano part in the room, and that alone was enough of a spark to build an entire song around. Sometimes it really is that simple — someone starts playing and the whole thing follows.
What's your take on rap beefs versus confessional songwriting being judged differently?

It's funny how rap beefs get framed as bold and confessional, but women being emotionally open in songs sometimes gets labeled as 'messy.' I think we should just treat it all as part of the same music conversation instead of singling out female artists.
Why do you think it's meaningful when male artists write emotionally vulnerable lyrics?

When a male artist writes something intensely confessional, it actually helps normalize that kind of honesty for everyone, including women. The more artists across the board lean into that vulnerability, the better it is for the whole culture around songwriting.
How do you feel about the phrase 'mess messaging in a bottle' describing your early songwriting?

Early on, songwriting was literally how I processed things I couldn't say to someone directly — especially when talking to them would've been unhealthy. The song became the place where that feeling could live safely instead of me acting on it.
What do you enjoy most about writing narrative-driven songs versus autobiographical ones?

It's genuinely thrilling trying to pack an entire plot into three and a half minutes so someone feels like they've experienced a whole story, almost like a bedtime tale. That challenge reshaped how I think about songwriting entirely.
How do you feel about writing an entire album inspired by challenging yourself creatively rather than reacting to public life?
That particular project wasn't really a response to fame or public scrutiny — it came from genuinely wanting to test myself as a writer and see what else I was capable of outside my usual lane.
What do you enjoy about writing from a narrator who isn't yourself?

I like stepping into a perspective that isn't mine to relate to directly — like playing an industry executive watching different women get told they're 'the next big thing,' which lets me explore a pattern without it being purely personal.
What do you think it means for the entertainment industry to 'love bomb' new talent?

It's this cycle where the industry showers someone with excitement and validation before really knowing who they are, then eventually discards them once the idealized version doesn't hold up. It's something I've both witnessed and lived through in different ways.
How do you feel about performing a heartbreaking song live after years of distance from writing it?
Watching fans respond so intensely to a song years after I wrote it made me almost emotional wondering what if it had never happened — what if that raw version never got shared at all.
What do you think fuels your most confessional songs?
A lot of my proudest songs exist specifically because of criticism I received about some part of my personality or choices. Without that friction, some of those songs simply wouldn't exist.
How did you first hear about your now-partner's on-air declaration on his podcast?

I'd actually been listening to that podcast for a while, so when he used it to shoot his shot, I already had context for who he was. It was a pretty bold move to fold a personal request into a football show like that.
What was your first reaction to being asked out publicly on a podcast?
At first I thought, this guy is being genuinely earnest and non-judgmental about not knowing anything about my world, and that honesty made me want to actually respond instead of brushing it off.
How did friends and family react once they found out someone was interested in you?

People started reaching out on his behalf almost immediately — cousins, mutual friends, even people connected to his coach — all vouching for him being genuinely great. That kind of unsolicited support isn't normal, so it stood out.
What was it like going to a football game for the first time to support someone?
I walked straight through general admission with everyone else, no separate entrance, nothing fancy. It felt oddly grounding — just blending into the crowd like anyone else there for the game.
How would you describe your dad's personality in social situations?
He's the most social person alive — he'll make a friend in an airport after a five-minute conversation and still be checking in with them years later. It's honestly a rare and impressive skill.
What happened when you learned a specific player got drafted onto the team you were now following?
I was literally running through the halls of my house screaming about a draft pick, and my friends had no idea what had happened to me — I'd become a completely different person overnight.
How did you first learn the rules of football?

I asked embarrassingly basic questions at the start, like assuming both quarterbacks were on the field simultaneously. I've come a long way since then, but I still wouldn't call myself ready to analyze coverages professionally.
What was the moment like when your partner joined you on stage during a tour stop?
We were half-joking about the idea backstage, and then I saw a little twinkle that told me he actually wanted to do it. The crowd reaction was one of the loudest of the entire tour.
What was going through his mind, according to him, being on stage in front of that many people?
He said he felt like the smallest speck of life up there — someone who's played in massive stadiums his whole career, completely humbled by the scale of a concert crowd.
What was it like watching him meet the royal family for the first time?

He had this whole internal debate about whether to bring his beer along or set it down out of respect, and watching him work through that etiquette question in real time was honestly one of the funniest things I've witnessed.
What was your first impression meeting his close friend group?
He moved through the room at what felt like lightning speed — one second here, gone the next, like watching an incredibly fast, oversized cricket. It was chaotic in the best way.
How does your dad describe recovering from major surgery?

He kept saying 'new arteries, new me' the whole recovery, staying weirdly upbeat and cracking jokes constantly, even comparing himself to how a cat curls around a tree to heal — which obviously doesn't apply to heart surgery, but it was very him.
What did the whole family do differently that summer because of the surgery?

We basically all moved in together for the summer to help him recover, taking shifts overnight in the hospital. It ended up being one of the most bonding stretches of time we've had as a family in years.
What's a funny detail about your dad's recovery in the hospital?
He came out of surgery and immediately tried handing out guitar picks to the nurses and doctors, despite not having pockets in his hospital gown. He was in genuinely great spirits the whole time.
How did you learn to make sourdough bread?
A family friend brought over an incredible loaf, and I basically insisted on learning immediately — she taught me the process, gave me some of her starter, and my life hasn't been the same since.
What kind of sourdough varieties have you experimented with?
Regular sourdough, blueberry lemon, cinnamon swirl, cinnamon raisin, and I've even been working on a rainbow funfetti version for the younger kids in the family who love anything with sprinkles.
How does your partner feel about your baking obsession?
He's fully along for the ride at this point — I've had him stretching and folding dough, though admittedly his loaf rose a little higher than mine, much to his delight.
What's your relationship with bread puns like these days?
Once I start, I genuinely cannot stop — the worse the pun, the better, in my opinion. It's become a whole side hobby layered onto the actual baking.
Why did you name a song's bridge after a Shakespeare reference?
I wanted to sprinkle in some paraphrased lines from Hamlet because the emotional core of the story — being saved from love driving you mad — mirrored what I was trying to say in that bridge.
Did you have to reread Hamlet before writing that reference?
Not really a full reread — I already knew the story well enough. I just did a small refresher to make sure the specific paraphrased lines landed the way I wanted.
What's the concept behind the album cover referencing a famous painting?

The cover nods to a well-known painting of a woman associated with drowning and lost love, and that reference carries through into the music video too, layered with even more nods to the same imagery.
Why did you decide to direct the music video yourself?

It was such a fun creative challenge to write and direct something with that many layered references myself, rather than handing that vision fully to someone else. Getting to shape every visual detail felt really rewarding.
How did it feel finally releasing new music after a period away?

There's this uncomplicated joy about it this time — no lingering tortured feeling like some past releases carried. My life just isn't in the same emotionally heavy place it was when writing those earlier records.
What's the story behind announcing music through cheeky lyrical references?

There's a lyric that plays with wordplay around a favorite baking show title, and I couldn't resist folding in a joke about baking right alongside a music reference — the timing just felt too perfect not to use.
What does 'keep it 100' mean in the context of your numerology habits?
It ties back to two meaningful numbers adding up to one hundred — a little nod to both of us and how those specific digits have shown up meaningfully in both our lives.
Why does one lyric reference feel 'Christmassy' to fans even though it's not a holiday song?

There's a sparkly, twinkly quality to the imagery in that line that naturally evokes a festive feeling, even though the song itself isn't seasonal at all. I think that's part of why fans picked up on the vibe.
What do you remember most fondly about your big stadium tour, emotionally?
I could see the crowd clearly from the stage every night, and that visible joy fed me even on the most exhausting nights. It fueled the whole run in a way I don't think people realize.
Do you miss touring life now that it's over?

Honestly, my body feels so much better now — joints recovering, hobbies coming back. Touring for years straight leaves very little room for anything else in life, so this pause has felt genuinely necessary.
Are you itching to go back on tour again soon?
Not really, not yet. If I'm going to do it again, I want to do it really well, and right now I'm just genuinely tired in a way that needs real recovery time before considering that again.
What was it like watching both your original and re-recorded songs get played side by side on the radio?

It became this fun moment where listeners would demand the re-recorded version specifically, which honestly probably helped move the whole masters negotiation forward. Their loyalty mattered more than people realize.
How do you feel now that radio stations can play either version freely?

It's genuinely funny to hear both versions blended together, honestly indistinguishable most of the time. I'm just glad listeners finally have the freedom to choose whichever version resonates with them.
What made this particular masters deal different from previous ownership attempts?

The people who owned my catalog this time were never obligated to sell it back to me at all — they didn't have to make that deal. The fact that they did, after hearing the personal story behind it, meant everything.
Why do you think it's important for other artists to know about ownership rights?

Not everyone will prioritize this the same way I did, and that's fine — but artists deserve to at least know the option exists before they sign a deal, so they can decide what actually matters to them.
What do you hope this whole masters ownership story inspires in other artists?

I hope it encourages people coming up now to at least ask the question and look into it for themselves, rather than assuming it's just how the industry has to work. If it matters to them, they should go after it.
Why do you avoid responding to people trying to provoke you online?

Engaging just gives that kind of provocation more oxygen — and honestly, some of it feels designed purely to bait a reaction for attention. I'd rather stay quiet on most of it and save my energy for things that actually matter.
What made you decide to speak up publicly about the masters situation specifically?

I don't speak out about many personal industry issues, but this one felt different — I felt like sharing it honestly could actually help other artists learn something valuable, so it was worth breaking my usual silence.
How did it feel seeing this story evolve into something bigger than just your own catalog?

It's still surreal that a personal, years-long frustration turned into something that's raised broader awareness across the industry about artist ownership. I'm incredibly grateful it played out that way, even though the process itself was often infuriating.
What do you think the biggest overlap is between performing on stage and playing professional football?

Both jobs boil down to entertaining people intensely for a few hours using an enormous amount of preparation beforehand. The terminology differs, but the core demand on your body and focus is remarkably similar.
How do you think about pressure differently now compared to earlier in your career?

I used to feel weighed down by pressure in a more anxious way, but now I try to treat it as a signal that something meaningful is actually happening — pressure means people care, and that's ultimately a good thing.
What was memorable about a fan interaction you had during your tour's final stretch?

By the final shows, fans had built their own elaborate traditions around specific songs, almost performing their own choreography in sync. It felt like we'd created something together that went beyond anything I could have scripted.
How do you feel about fans creating unplanned rituals during your shows?
It genuinely delights me every time — there's something really special about not knowing exactly how a crowd will respond and then watching them build a tradition entirely on their own.
What's something you learned about yourself through the physical demands of a long tour?
I learned just how far I could push my body and mind if I set my sights on a specific goal ahead of time, rather than letting stamina build gradually over the run like in past tours.
Why did you choose to exit the stage differently on the very last night of the tour?
I wanted to leave a subtle hint that I was closing one chapter while opening a new one — a small visual cue for fans who pay close attention, tied to a color that would matter for what came next.
How did fans react to noticing that final subtle change during the last show?
I was genuinely proud they caught it — it confirmed that the attention to detail I put into these things doesn't go unnoticed, even in a small gesture buried in a much bigger night.
What does the color choice for your new album era represent to you?
It reflects the emotional tone I was living in during that period — vibrant, energetic, a little wild, in a way that felt true to how my life actually looked and felt behind the scenes.
How did you decide on the title for your newest album?

It came from genuinely living that exact experience while I was writing it — flying back and forth between shows and songwriting sessions, fully immersed in performing while also creating something new in the background.
What made this particular songwriting partnership feel different from past collaborations?
This time, all three of us in the room were carrying equal creative weight — no longer a mentor-student dynamic, but genuine peers pushing each other after years apart working on separate projects.
What conversation set the tone for making this new album?

One of my collaborators told me he didn't want the storytelling instinct from my more narrative work to disappear just because we were leaning into catchier, more upbeat songs — and I promised him it never could, even if I tried.
What was your goal in choosing which photographers to work with for this album's visuals?
I went back to photographers I'd loved working with on a previous, more dramatic album era, because I wanted that same striking visual quality applied to this completely different emotional tone.
How do you decide when an album feels complete and ready to release?
When every song feels essential to the whole, like pieces of a puzzle that couldn't be swapped out or added to without changing the entire picture, that's when I know it's genuinely finished.
What's it like reconnecting with an old friend for a casual radio catch-up?

It's genuinely one of my favorite kinds of conversations — no real agenda, just two people who've known each other a long time catching up like normal. Those chats always feel overdue in the best way.
Why did you bring homemade bread to a radio interview?
I'd been deep into a baking phase and wanted to share a batch — lemon blueberry that time. It felt like a fun, low-key way to say hello before getting into the actual interview.
How do you feel about pun-covered packaging on things you make?
I love it — the cheesier the pun, the better as far as I'm concerned. It's become a bit of a signature at this point, sneaking wordplay onto anything I hand out.
What's the story behind a costume idea you floated for a big red carpet event?
I joked about showing up dressed as a robot carrying a sword, just to keep people guessing about what I'd actually wear. Half the fun of those events is not telegraphing the look ahead of time.
What's your favorite piece of slang your fans came up with on their own?
The nickname they gave themselves is honestly one of my favorite things — completely their invention, nothing I suggested. It's adorable that it caught on organically.
How do you feel about people reading your old diary entries publicly?

It's honestly hilarious looking back — teenage me complaining about school disappointments or being nasty because my mom picked me up late. There's a real comedy in how seriously I took everything back then.
What did you write in your diary about not having a boyfriend on Valentine's Day as a teenager?
I wrote something dramatically resigned about not acknowledging the holiday at all since I didn't have a crush or boyfriend to celebrate with — very teenage-angst, but it makes me laugh now.
What's a funny moment from being backstage before opening a big show?
I let it slip on air that I was opening a show before I was supposed to announce it, and immediately realized I probably wasn't meant to say that yet. Live TV moments like that just happen sometimes.
What's the story behind a funny mix-up over a text from a friend?

Someone insisted a friend had texted me, and there was this whole back-and-forth about who exactly the message was really meant for. It turned into one of those silly, circular arguments that's funnier in the moment than it sounds after.
What's a memorable line from filming a music video kissing scene?

I had to redo a kissing scene multiple times for different camera angles, and by the end I genuinely couldn't tell you how it felt anymore — it just became a technical exercise rather than anything romantic.
How do you handle paparazzi trying to get an unflattering photo?
I once hid under an umbrella specifically to ruin a paparazzi shot, joking that they'd have a hard time selling a picture of just an umbrella. Small victories against invasive photography.
What's your take on writing songs about people who ask you not to?
People will beg me not to write about them during a breakup, and then, well, I usually do anyway. It's become a running joke at this point — they should know better by now.
What's it like watching your own younger self talk about writing breakup songs?
There's something both funny and true about how consistently I've turned personal moments into songs, even when specifically asked not to. It's just always been part of how I process things.
What's the origin of the rhyme you made up about being single?
Someone asked if I was single, and I spontaneously came up with a little rhyme pairing it with a silly word — something I claimed on the spot to have invented right there.
How do you feel about people trying to come up with couple nicknames for you?
It's always funny watching people try to smash two names together into something catchy. I usually just play along and see what ridiculous combination they land on.
What's a funny bit about people comparing you to a supermodel?
Someone once told me I had the cheekbones for it, and I joked back that I'm actually more of an edgy mess underneath a fairly clean public image. The contrast always makes people laugh.
What happened when someone tried to teach you Irish phrases on the spot?
I was suspicious the whole time that they were secretly teaching me something inappropriate disguised as a greeting. Turned out to be harmless, but I made them swear on it first.
How would you react to being asked if you use dating apps?
I genuinely had to have someone explain how swiping works to me — it's just not been part of my world, though I get the concept is fun for the banter value alone.
What's a memorable line about temptation involving a mini bar setup?
Someone joked that if they ever got into my bedroom and found chocolate bars and drinks stocked up, they'd never leave — and honestly, that tracks, it sounds like a great setup.
What's your reaction to being told your fashion has leveled up recently?
That's honestly one of my favorite compliments — knowing someone's been quietly tracking the fashion evolution and putting together mental collages makes all the effort feel noticed.
How do you respond when someone jokes about wanting to carry you around in their purse?
I take it as the sweetest kind of compliment, honestly — it usually comes paired with someone calling you honest or vulnerable, which means more to me than almost anything else they could say.
What's it like being serenaded unexpectedly during an interview?
It's a rare, sweet surprise — I usually end up wanting to jump in on the background vocals myself before they've even finished the first line.
How do you feel when someone forgets to invite you to their wedding?
I always play it off as a joke — pretending to be offended that my invite got 'lost in the mail' even though I know it's all in good fun between old friends.
What's a funny bit about someone asking to be your date to an event?
When someone asks if we should date as a joke, I usually just lean into it playfully, calling them charming right back, because the banter is more fun than taking it seriously.
What's it like being asked to prove you have a 'flaw' on camera?
Someone once pushed me to admit something to make them feel better about themselves in comparison, and I ended up joking that I just sit alone watching crime shows most nights — hardly glamorous.
What's the story behind your character prep for playing a role in a stage musical?
There was a specific behavioral training class for the production that helped performers embody feline movement and mannerisms — it was an unusual, intensive kind of prep I hadn't done before.
How did you first hear about being offered that role?

After doing a screen test years earlier for a different musical and not getting it, this opportunity came as a straightforward offer instead of another audition process — a nice surprise given the earlier disappointment.
What's a funny wish you've made about awards or recognition?
I've joked that I really want an honorary doctorate someday, mostly because a friend of mine already has one and I feel like he looks down on me a little for not having one myself.
What's the deal with the royalties joke about a beloved sitcom theme song?
If I could've written any song, I half-joke it would be a famous sitcom theme song purely for how well those royalties must pay out over decades of reruns.
What's your favorite currently-airing TV show?
I've been really into a true-crime style news show lately — it's become a bit of a nightly comfort watch for me.
What's your all-time favorite TV show?
An old ensemble sitcom about a group of friends in New York — total comfort watch, I could rewatch it endlessly.
What's the most adventurous thing you've ever done, according to you?
Honestly, watching a nature documentary series about sharks is about as adventurous as I get — I'll leave the actual swimming with sharks to other people.
What kind of advice would you give someone about their upcoming anniversary trip?
When someone asked me for a recommendation on the spot, I suggested a scenic coastal spot — confidently, even if it was a bit of an improvised answer.
What's your go-to birthday cake memory?
A cake from a specific bakery for a milestone birthday was so good that even a famous friend at the party raved about it afterward — that's how I know it was genuinely great.
What was the last thing you baked before a big interview?
A gluten-free, dairy-free chocolate cake — trying to accommodate different dietary needs while still keeping it decadent.
How would you describe your spirit animal and why?
I'd say a dolphin, mainly because they're so social and travel in groups — that pack mentality feels very true to how I move through life with my friends.
What's a funny story about giving unsolicited relationship advice to a stranger?
Someone asked me on the spot where to take his wife for their anniversary, and I confidently recommended a beautiful coastal spot before adding a cheeky 'you're welcome' just for fun.
What's it like being asked to demonstrate a talent on camera repeatedly across different interviews?
I always default to the same double-jointed elbow trick because it's genuinely the only thing I've got — I've accepted that it's not impressive, just mildly startling.
How do you feel about being asked how many cat breeds you can name quickly?
I actually surprised myself rattling off several breeds under time pressure — clearly all those hours around cats have paid off in trivia knowledge.
What's the coolest thing in your songwriting room, according to you?
I'd say the fireplace, honestly — it just makes the whole space feel warmer and more inviting for actually sitting down and writing.
Whose closet would you raid if you could raid anyone's?
A specific actress friend of mine with an incredible sense of style — her wardrobe would be an absolute dream to go through.
What's your favorite fashion trend of all time?
High-waisted everything — it's timeless to me and something I keep coming back to no matter what era of fashion is trending.
What's an essential item you always carry in your purse?
A lavender antibacterial hand spray — I bring it out anytime we're waiting in line for a public bathroom sink, and my friends always appreciate it.
What's the funniest reaction you've gotten pulling out hand sanitizer in public?
My friends always end up thanking me mid-eye-roll because it's such a small, practical thing, but genuinely useful every single time we're out.
What's a moment where you got playfully called out for being 'too on brand'?
Someone joked that a scrabble word I played was suspiciously convenient, and I insisted it was completely spontaneous — spontaneity is basically part of my personality, or so I claimed.
What do you think about people Googling themselves?
My honest advice after doing it myself is: don't. You should never Google yourself — it rarely does anything good for your mental state.
Why do you think you're one of the most followed people on a certain platform?
I joke that it's entirely because my cats are adorable — which honestly might not be that far from the truth given how much people love a good cat post.
What's the story behind gifting an olive tree as a Christmas present?
Someone once planted an olive tree in my yard for Christmas, and it remains one of the most thoughtful, unexpected gifts I've ever received.
What nickname does your family use for you?
My brother calls me a specific childhood nickname that's stuck around — it's a small, sweet reminder of home whenever he uses it.
What message do you think young girls are getting from pop culture today that concerns you?

I don't think it's healthy that girls are taught being edgy, sexy, or cool matters more than anything else. Those messages get pushed down from the media into schools, and honestly, I've never really fit into any of those boxes myself.
When have you felt like you weren't good enough?

Mostly during times when I was being fed the idea that being edgy or cool was the standard to live up to. My life just doesn't naturally gravitate that way, and for a while that made me feel like I was falling short.
