How would you describe yourself, if not edgy or cool?
I'd say imaginative, smart, and hardworking — qualities that honestly don't get prioritized nearly enough in pop culture compared to how they should be.
What do you imagine saying to your future partner about past heartbreak?

I used to think that once I found the right person, I genuinely wouldn't be able to remember the guy who broke up with me over the phone in twenty-five seconds when I was eighteen. Funny how some memories just fade once something better replaces them.
What's your favorite memory from filming a music video?
Filming a particular music video where the visual concept and choreography came together really cinematically is genuinely one of my favorite career memories — it just felt like everything clicked.
How did you feel putting out an album you knew some people wouldn't immediately embrace?
I remember telling myself people could say whatever they wanted — I knew what I'd made and I loved it. It didn't matter if it took years for everyone else to come around to feeling the same way.
What does it feel like writing a song you're excited about even before you know how it'll land?

There's this specific feeling — almost like wanting to headbang through a wall — that happens when I'm writing something I love so much I stop caring whether anyone else gets it. I've chased that feeling in the studio more than once.
What's the 'rant bridge' concept you and your collaborator came up with, with specific examples?

It's this stream-of-consciousness burst of emotion we love building into songs — you can hear it in tracks where the bridge turns into this intense, escalating outpour that we usually bring back again before the final chorus.
What's it like sharing a birthday season with your fans?
It's fun when a whole cluster of birthdays lands in the same window with people close to you — there's something nice about that shared astrological season connecting you to people you care about.
How do you respond when a fan asks something as simple as 'how do you do it all'?

That kind of question always catches me off guard in the best way — I usually just say I feel incredibly lucky to get to do what I love, and even luckier that people care enough about the lyrics for me to keep playing with how I release music.
What's a touching message you've received from a fan on air?
Someone once told me how grateful they were that I existed and that I kept making music — that kind of unprompted gratitude from a stranger always catches me off guard emotionally.
How do you feel when longtime fans say they think about your tour every day?
It means so much to hear that, honestly — I think about it constantly too. I had so much fun on that run, physically brutal as it was, that it's stayed with me just as vividly.
How could you tell what the crowd was experiencing from the stage?

I actually have really strong eyesight after getting corrective surgery, so I could see for miles past the stadium during shows — watching that joy from the crowd fed me even on the most exhausting nights.
What's the concept behind the song built around a power-dynamic metaphor and a title reference to fatherhood?

It plays with the idea of a 'father figure' as a stand-in for larger power structures, flipping who actually holds control in that dynamic. It's a very different use of that phrase than people might expect.
How do you feel about your dad's role modeling social skills for you growing up?
He's the most naturally social person I've ever known — able to build real relationships out of the briefest interactions. It's a talent I don't think I could replicate even if I tried.
What do you think when someone highlights a lyric that took real emotional courage to write?

I always appreciate when someone points out a specific line that clearly mattered to write, even if I can't always talk through every detail of what it meant. Some lines are just deeply personal in ways I keep close.
What do you enjoy about numerology beyond just favorite numbers?
I just genuinely love math patterns and dates lining up in unexpected ways — it scratches a specific itch for me, finding structure and meaning in numbers that most people would just breeze past.
What happened during one of your award show wins tied to the number thirteen?

At one particular award show, right before I went on, a producer told me we'd be starting in exactly thirteen seconds — completely coincidentally lining up with a number that's followed me my whole career.
How do you view the idea of 'keeping it 100' with someone close to you?
There's a fun bit of numerology where two specific ages add up to a hundred, which became a little inside reference between us — a playful way of tying our numbers together.
What was the most extensive creative restoration project you've ever undertaken on a song?

Piecing together a longer, original version of a song from old diaries and fragments was easily the most extensive restoration I've ever done — searching for lost verses and reconstructing something that had existed only in memory for years.
What do you think about people who assume every emotional song must be about heartbreak from a specific relationship?
People love to play detective with lyrics, trying to pin every emotional song to a specific person, but I wrote it — that ownership belongs to me regardless of who or what inspired a single line.
How do you feel about people trying to guess who inspired a song before it's even released?

It gets a little strange when it starts to feel like people are trying to run a paternity test on a song. At the end of the day, the song is mine, shaped by my perspective, regardless of who or what sparked it.
What's a piece of advice you give new artists about handling online criticism specifically?

I always ask them why they're reading their own comments in the first place — that kind of unfocused criticism rarely helps. If something legitimately sparks a creative idea, use it, but don't let it edit who you are as an artist.
What do you think makes criticism sometimes a gift rather than just a hurdle?

If a piece of criticism genuinely makes you think and gives you something to respond to creatively, that's a gift — maybe you wouldn't have written that song otherwise. But that's different from letting trolls dictate your mood.
How do you approach writing about love that feels genuinely uncomplicated for once?

This time around, the songs about love don't carry that twisted, tortured undertone from before — it's bright and happy, though it still has some teeth to it, because real love isn't just sugary sweetness.
What do you mean when you say a song has 'teeth' even if it's happy?

Even the brightest, happiest songs on this album still carry some edge or bite to them — real life always has some element of drama sneaking in, which makes for more interesting songwriting than pure sweetness alone.
What do you think has changed about how you write love songs now compared to earlier albums?

Writing from a place of genuine happiness hasn't made me lose access to the harder emotions — frustration, anger, even old wounds are still available to draw from. Being settled just gave me a wider range to pull from creatively.
How did your songwriting partners feel returning to work together after years apart?

We'd all been off honing different skills separately, so by the time we reunited, everyone brought a new level of sharpness. It felt less like old habits and more like meeting each other again as more developed artists.
What pressure did you put on yourself going into this new album creatively?

I told my collaborator I wanted this album to make me as proud as the tour did, for the same reasons — ambition, scale, care in every detail. He immediately pointed out how much pressure that put on us, and honestly, he was right.
What genre-leaning albums did you make in between big pop eras, and how did that shape you?
I spent time making more alt-folk leaning records that let me explore restraint and subtlety as a writer. That experience gave me more tools to bring back into bigger, catchier songwriting later.
How do you know when a song idea deserves a spot on a tightly curated album versus being left off?

If I can honestly say every song exists for a clear reason and the album wouldn't feel whole without it, that's the bar. This time I wanted twelve songs that each earned their place, rather than a larger, looser collection.
What was your emotional state like specifically while making this new album during the tour?

I'd play three tour shows in a row, then fly somewhere else entirely to work on new music during days off, then head back to the tour. Physically I was exhausted, but mentally I was more stimulated and excited than I'd been in a while.
What's the significance of choosing a briefcase to reveal your new album?
I like matching the reveal method to the spirit of the album itself — playful, a little theatrical, unexpected. It fits the tone of what's inside far better than a conventional announcement would.
Why did you want a featured collaborator on the title track of your new album?
Having someone else's voice join on the final title track felt like the right way to close out the record — a way of extending the showgirl concept beyond just my own perspective.
What's the emotional arc you hope listeners feel across this whole new album?

I want it to feel like living through the exuberant, electric backstage version of my life during that period — joyful, dramatic, occasionally messy, but ultimately full of genuine excitement rather than heaviness.
How do you feel about people saying this new album is a tonal 180 from your last one?

That's honestly the point — every album I make tries to do something completely different from the one before it. This one mirrors exactly where my life is right now, which happens to be a much more upbeat chapter.
What do you think listeners will notice first about this new record compared to your last one?

The tone shift will probably be the most obvious thing — where the last album was purely about lyrical catharsis, this one leans into infectious melody and joy first, without losing the specificity in the writing.
Why did you want the album cover to feel like an off-stage moment rather than a performance shot?

Because this album isn't really about what happened under the lights each night — it's about everything happening behind the curtain emotionally. Showing the after felt more honest to what the songs are actually about.
What can you tell us about the photography style for this album's visuals?

I wanted to keep some mystery around the full visual rollout for now, but the photographers I worked with are ones I've loved collaborating with before on a different, more dramatic era — so expect that same striking quality applied to a very different mood.
What made you confident enough to reveal your whole tracklist on the spot?
Once the album felt complete and every song had earned its place, I wanted to just show the whole thing rather than drip-feed pieces — it felt more exciting to reveal it all in one confident moment.
How would you sum up the difference between your last two albums in one sentence each?
One was a full catharsis, page after page of raw poetry. This one is joy set to melody — vivid, focused, and meant to feel the way an electric, exuberant chapter of life actually feels.
What do you hope people take away from hearing this album for the first time?

I hope it feels like catching a genuinely joyful, high-energy season of someone's life in real time — melodies that get stuck in your head and lyrics specific enough that you feel like you actually lived it alongside me.
Why do you think this collaboration with longtime producers felt different this time around?

We'd all grown independently in the years apart, so coming back together felt less like picking up where we left off and more like starting fresh with sharper instincts and more trust in each other's ideas.
What do you think makes an album cohesive rather than just a collection of songs?

Cohesion comes from every song serving the same emotional through-line, so removing or adding a track would actually break the shape of the whole thing. That kind of intentional structure was the main goal this time.
How do you personally define success for this new chapter of your career?
Success this time isn't about scale or numbers — it's about being as proud of the album as I am of the tour, for the same reasons: ambition, detail, and genuinely loving every piece of it.
What do you want fans to feel emotionally listening to this record for the first time?
I want them to feel the same electric, joyful energy I was living in while making it — like they got a real glimpse behind the curtain of a genuinely happy, high-stakes chapter of my life.
How does this album fit into the larger arc of your career so far?

It feels like the culmination of years of experimenting across genres and moods — bringing back the sharpest pop instincts from earlier eras while carrying forward the storytelling discipline I picked up from more narrative records in between.
What's the story behind the honorary doctorate speech you gave?

I packed that commencement speech with so many lyrical Easter eggs that when my next album came out, fans realized the entire speech had basically been one long hint. That kind of layered reveal is exactly the kind of thing I love pulling off.
How do you feel about doing something for the first time on a podcast, career-wise?
It was genuinely exciting being somewhere brand new after twenty years in the industry — there's not a lot of true firsts left, so getting to experience one felt special.
What was the moment like calling your mom right after you found out about the masters deal?
She was the one who actually called me with the news, and I just hit the floor. I couldn't process it standing up — total disbelief mixed with years of hope finally landing somewhere real.
How did you first tell your partner the news about getting your masters back?

He was in the middle of playing video games, and I tried to walk in and say it calmly, but I ended up completely breaking down instead. He immediately dropped everything the second he realized something big had happened.
What was one of the most physically demanding parts of performing every night on tour?

Keeping the show at the same length and intensity every single night, regardless of how I felt physically, was brutal. You can't just decide to shorten the show because you're sick — the fans' experience always came first.
How did meeting people from a completely different industry change your perspective on pressure?

Watching how much pressure professional athletes carry into every single game gave me a whole new respect for that world. It reframed how I think about my own pressure, knowing there are other high-stakes performance jobs out there built very differently from mine.
What do you think it says about someone when they're unfazed by an unfamiliar world?

When someone doesn't need everything explained or curated for them and just dives in without judgment, that tells you a lot about their character. That kind of ease made a big difference early in getting to know each other.
What's something you learned about football specifically that surprised you?

I didn't realize how positionally specific everything is — like assuming quarterbacks from both teams were on the field together. Learning the actual structure of the game turned out to be way more interesting than I expected.
How do you feel about being an outsider who fell in love with a completely new world?

There's something really fun about becoming genuinely obsessed with something you knew nothing about a year prior. I went from confused bystander to someone actively following draft picks and cheering with real investment.
What do you think makes a long-term creative partnership last?
Trust and enough time apart to grow independently, so that when you come back together, you're not just repeating old patterns — you're bringing something new to push each other further.
What do you think is the value of stepping away from a familiar creative team for a while?
Taking time apart to explore different sounds or styles ends up making the eventual reunion sharper — everyone comes back with new tools and perspective instead of falling into old habits.
How do you approach picking which stories from your life to turn into songs?
It's not really a conscious selection process — usually the story that won't leave me alone is the one that ends up becoming a song, whether it's mine or borrowed from someone else's history.
What's the emotional difference for you between a song that comes out fast versus one that takes years?

The fast ones usually come from ideas I've already been sitting with for a while, so the writing itself is quick. The slow ones tend to be the emotionally heavier songs I have to keep setting down and picking back up.
What do you think it means to write from behind a character rather than directly as yourself?

It gives you permission to explore a feeling fully without it being read as a literal confession. Sometimes the emotional truth comes through more clearly when it's filtered through someone else's story.
How has your relationship with public criticism evolved over two decades in the industry?
Two decades in, it's genuinely hard to rattle me the way it once could. I've built enough distance and perspective that most noise just doesn't stick the way it might have earlier in my career.
What do you think is underappreciated about long-term friendships in a public life?

Having people who've known you since before any of the fame or scrutiny existed is incredibly grounding. They treat you the same regardless of what's happening publicly, which is rarer than people realize.
